EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 â†’ 4.6, 4.6 â†’ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll execute the Refactor phase autonomously. Let me start by reading the architecture notes and current code.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.
5	
6	## After cycle 2
7	- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
8	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base Ă— first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000â€¦01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
9	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
13	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers â€” speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
14	- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table â€” zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again â€” still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.
18	
19	## After cycle 5
20	- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` â†’ `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup â†’ item-modifier multiplier â†’ first-insurance loading + admin fee).
21	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` â€” having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
22	- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.
23

content.text:
Now I have full context. Let me evaluate the current code carefully.

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**

- `runScenario` â€” matches contract, stays.
- `item`, `cursedMultiplier`, `highEnchantmentMultiplier` â€” clear.
- `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` â€” captures that this applies for high-level enchantments (>=5). Good.
- `itemBase` â€” represents the per-item priced amount after item-modifiers. Acceptable but slightly ambiguous (it's no longer "base"; it's "base Ă— modifiers"). However, given upcoming multi-item changes, this name will likely get replaced soon. Keep for now.

**Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass**

```
function runScenario(scenario: unknown): unknown {
  const { steps } = scenario as Scenario;                                  // 1 binding (steps); 1 binding (scenario param)
  const item = steps[0].items[0];                                           // bindings: item; constants: 0, 0; invocations: 0 (just member access)
  const cursedMultiplier = item.cursed ? CURSED_MULTIPLIER : 1;            // binding + conditional + constant 1
  const highEnchantmentMultiplier =
    (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= 5 ? HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER : 1;        // binding + conditional + constants 0, 5, 1; invocation (??, >=)
  const itemBase =
    BASE_PRICE[item.type] * cursedMultiplier * highEnchantmentMultiplier;  // binding + invocations (Ă—, Ă—)
  const premium = Math.round(itemBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE; // binding + invocations (round, Ă—, +)
  return { results: [{ premium }] };                                        // (object literal)
}
```

Rough count:
- Bindings: scenario, steps, item, cursedMultiplier, highEnchantmentMultiplier, itemBase, premium (~7) + references = many. Just count bindings introduced (~7) and refs as needed.
- Constants: 0, 1, 0, 5, 1 = 5
- Invocations (Ă—, ??, >=, Ă—, Ă—, round, Ă—, +): ~8 Ă— 2 = 16
- Conditionals: 2 Ă— 4 = 8
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

Total ~36. Within ballpark of cycle 5's ~34.

**Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules**

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** Let me confirm.

content.text:
Tests green.

**Rule 2 (Reveal Intent):** The two-modifier pattern is clear. Both follow `<condition> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`. The architecture notes from cycle 5 explicitly said: *"the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live"* â€” and now we have the second modifier. This is the duplication driver.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** YES â€” `cursedMultiplier` and `highEnchantmentMultiplier` are sibling patterns: each is "if some condition on item â†’ use multiplier constant, else 1", and they're multiplied together. With two siblings now visible, extracting an `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` helper that combines all item-condition multipliers is justified.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Extracting one helper adds one function but removes two named locals and combines them more cleanly.

**Step 4: Implement Refactoring**

Extract `itemModifierMultiplier(item)`. This:
- Names the pricing stage that combines all item-attribute multipliers.
- Removes the two sibling locals that read like copy-paste.
- Creates a clean place for future item-modifiers (anti-curse? dragon-material? high-enchantment in claims?) to live.
- Keeps `runScenario` body readable as a pipeline.

content.text: Tests still green. Marking task 5 complete.

**Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass**

`itemModifierMultiplier`:
- Bindings: item param, cursed, highEnchantment (3)
- Constants: 1, 0, 5, 1 (4)
- Invocations: ??, >=, Ă— (return) (3 Ă— 2 = 6)
- Conditionals: 2 (Ă— 4 = 8)

Subtotal ~21.

`runScenario`:
- Bindings: scenario, steps, item, itemPrice, premium (5)
- Constants: (none new â€” just literal indices `0` count but those existed before too)
- Invocations: Ă—, itemModifierMultiplier(), Ă—, round, + (5 Ă— 2 = 10)
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0

Subtotal ~17. Total ~38.

Mass: **~36 â†’ ~38**. Slight increase from the function call overhead, but Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) trumps APP. The pipeline stage "apply item-attribute multipliers" is now named, and `runScenario` reads top-to-bottom as: lookup base â†’ apply item modifiers â†’ apply first-insurance loading + admin fee.

**Step 6: Document Decision**

Refactoring applied:
- Extracted `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` â€” names the second stage of the pricing pipeline.
- Renamed `itemBase` â†’ `itemPrice` (more accurate; it's no longer just the base after modifiers applied).
- Rule 3 satisfied: the two sibling `<condition> ? MULTIPLIER : 1` snippets are now co-located, ready for cycle-7's combined cursed+enchantment test which just exercises them together (no edits needed).
- Rule 2: the pricing pipeline is now visible at a glance in `runScenario`.
- Slight mass increase (~36 â†’ ~38) is acceptable; clarity wins.

**Step 8: Update architecture notes**

input.new_string:
## After cycle 5
- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` â†’ `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup â†’ item-modifier multiplier â†’ first-insurance loading + admin fee).
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` â€” having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.

## After cycle 6
- Change: added `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER = 1.3`; extracted helper `itemModifierMultiplier(item: Item): number` that combines `cursed` and high-enchantment factors (each `<cond> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`, multiplied together); renamed `itemBase` â†’ `itemPrice`; `runScenario` body collapses to base lookup â†’ item-modifier multiplier â†’ first-insurance loading + admin fee, with the modifier stage now a single named call.
- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) finally has a driver â€” the second sibling modifier landed in cycle 6, matching the cycle-5 prediction, so the speculative extraction resisted then is now justified. Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming the "apply all item-attribute multipliers" stage makes the pricing pipeline scannable; `itemPrice` is more accurate than `itemBase` once modifiers have been applied. APP rose slightly (~36 â†’ ~38) from the function-call frame, but Rule 2 > APP per project conventions. Cycle 7 (cursed AND enchantment 5) will exercise both branches together with zero edits to `runScenario` body â€” both factors will simply multiply inside the helper. Cycle-7's `ceil(...)` in the spec formula (220 vs would-be-rounded 220) will need attention: current `Math.round` may give same answer for that specific test by coincidence (100Ă—1.5Ă—1.3Ă—1.1 = 214.5 â†’ roundâ†’215, ceilâ†’215; +5 = 220 either way... actually round(214.5)=214 banker's rounding in some langs, but JS Math.round = 215; ceil = 215; both yield 220), so cycle 7 may pass with no change; cycle 9 (`ceil(60 Ă— 0.80 Ă— 1.10) + 5 = 58`; product = 52.8, both round and ceil â†’ 53, +5 = 58 â€” also coincidence-passes). The real ceil-vs-round driver only appears when loyalty discount lands on a non-`.5` fractional that diverges; defer the roundâ†’ceil switch until a test actually fails.
- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere â€” claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Refactoring**: Extracted `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` helper that co-locates the two sibling `<condition> ? MULTIPLIER : 1` modifier factors (cursed, high-enchantment) and multiplies them. Renamed `itemBase` â†’ `itemPrice`.

**Mass Change**: ~36 â†’ ~38 (slight increase from function-call frame, accepted because Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) trumps APP â€” the pricing pipeline is now `base lookup â†’ item modifiers â†’ first-insurance loading + admin fee`, each stage one named element).

**Naming Evaluation**: `runScenario` (contract-fixed), `item`, `itemPrice`, `itemModifierMultiplier`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` all reveal intent at the right level of granularity.

**Rules Applied**:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): 6 passed, 18 todo â€” green before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): pipeline stages now named.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): cycle 5 predicted "when a third modifier arrives, extract"; cycle 6 added the second, then noticed the duplication driver is already present with two siblings, so extracted now per cycle-5's underlying intent.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): added one helper function but removed two named locals from the calling scope and tightened `runScenario` body from 6 lines to 4.

**Tests**: All 6 passing.

Relevant files:
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/architecture-notes.md

Proceeding to the next test (cycle 7: cursed AND enchantment 5 sword â†’ 220).
